name: Central Laser Facility
description: The CLF is one of the world’s leading centres for multi-disciplinary science and innovation using lasers. It is one of the three open access UK National Facilities that works with a national and international user Community of research scientists that are operating at the forefront of their respective fields and require access to the most advanced and sophisticated laser based facilities available.    Its work spans the scientific spectrum, working with the majority of UK universities, all five UK scientific Research Councils, industry, charities, other agencies of the state such as the MoD, the NHS, the Home Office and a wide range international partners including many EU Universities / institutes, the European Commission and other European national funding agencies.     The CLF houses some of the world's most powerful laser facilities for high energy density / high intensity science (Vulcan) and plasma based acceleration (Gemini). It’s large scale imaging facilities (Octopus) are amongst the most advanced laser based microscopy and nanoscopy systems available anywhere and its facilities for dynamics research (Ultra, Artemis) operate in the femto- and atto-second domain.     The CLF provides critical support functions to its Community, particularly in theoretical and computations physics, advanced micro/nano fabrication of targetry and engineering. Access to all CLF facilities is independently peer reviewed and free at the point of delivery.     The CLF has a rich history of innovation and societal / economic impact over several decades. From its earliest days, the origins of laser eye surgery for corneal reshaping, now commonplace on the high street, can be formally traced back to early experiments in the CLF. It’s latest spinout, Cobalt Light Systems Ltd, has commercialised CLF patented techniques for through barrier detection and developed a range of products for detecting liquid explosives that have seen widespread uptake by major airports throughout Europe. Over its history, the CLF has been involved in the formation of more than 10 spinouts and one third of all the patents STFC hold across its portfolio have originated in the CLF.     The CLF operates a technology transfer centre (CALTA - The Centre for Advanced Laser Technology and Applications) to facilitate the commercial exploitation of its advanced diode laser technology and to pioneer new and novel applications of extreme photonics to the benefit of wider society.

description: We operate three separate labs with several setups dedicated to quantum optics and spintronics experiments at cryogenic temperatures. name: TARANIS Laser facility
description: TARANIS (Terawatt Apparatus for Relativistic and Non-linear Interdisciplinary Science) is a Nd:glass laser system, which delivers 30 Joule/1.5 nanosecond pulses in each of two beamlines , which are compressible (via Chirped Pulse Amplification techniques) to ~ 20Joule/0.5 picosecond, for a complessive power of 80 TW. The laser pulses can be delivered delivered to two fully equipped target areas, complete of interaction chambers and an extensive set of diagnostics: optical (16 bits, large-chip CCD cameras, optical streak cameras), X-rays (range of X-UV and x-ray spectrometers) and particle diagnostics/detectors (Thomson parabolas/magnetic spectrometers, Micro-Channel Plates, Image Plate and RCF scanners). The system supports a range of experimental activities (including several EPSRC-supported programmes) all based around high-power laser interactions with matter and the applications of secondary sources from this interaction. Supported activities include laser-driven ion acceleration, pulsed radiobiology and radiolysis, warm dense matter studies, development of pulsed x-ray sources, electron transport studies. The facility is also used for training early career researchers, and for precursor experiments and preliminary tests leading to follow-up activities in larger laser facilities, such as the Central Laser Facility at the Rutherford Appleton Laboratory. TARANIS is currently being upgraded, with funding from EPSRC, with the addition of an ultrashort beamline (10 fs, initially at 10s of mJ energy level). TARANIS is the largest University-based system in Europe in its category, with largest systems only available in national facilities/ large research laboratories. The power and intensity by TARANIS are significant enough to allow for internationally competitive research, while, compared to large facilities, where competitive access is typically available only for a few weeks at a time, the TARANIS system offers opportunities for extended investigations with less time pressure. As mentioned above, such a system is also essential for preparatory studies and PhD training.

name: Time and Quantum Frequency Standards -  National Physical Laboratory
description: Time and frequency - The home of UK timeWe provide customers and partners with access to trusted and reliable time, and push the boundaries of timing technologies. NPL operates the UK primary frequency standards and uses these to contribute to global timekeeping. We also create the UK national timescale (UTC(NPL) and disseminate accurate time and frequency to users across the UK, via services such as the MSF radio time signal, our internet time service, and NPLTime®.Atomic timekeeping underpins technologies that are part of our daily lives, such as mobile phones, the internet and global navigation satellite systems. We carry out a broad programme of research in time and frequency metrology to support the social and industrial applications of precise timing. We are developing and characterising a new generation of optical atomic clocks which are based on laser-cooled trapped ions and atoms. We participate in European collaborations to verify the international consistency of these new clocks.Optical atomic clocks have the potential to improve satellite navigation systems and measurements of the Earth's gravity potential, as well as test fundamental physical theories. This work is expected to lead to a redefinition of the SI unit of time, the second.Building on this capability, we are developing compact and user-friendly quantum clocks / frequency standards for applications in navigation, high-speed communications, security and defence. We are also applying the techniques that underpin atomic clocks to develop new quantum sensors such as atomic magnetometers, gravity gradiometers and inertial sensors. Our researchers carry out further time and frequency measurement research, including microphotonics for metrology and sensors, and trace gas detection.NPL, as the UK's National Measurement Institute, is the prime deliverer of the UK Measurement Strategy. NPL is a world-leading centre of excellence in developing and applying the most accurate measurement standards, science and technology available. For more than a century, NPL has developed and maintained the nation's primary measurement standards. These standards underpin an infrastructure of traceability throughout the UK and the world that ensures accuracy and consistency of measurement.Keywords:time & frequency, compact atomic clocks, magnetometry, time and frequency distribution, seismic detection, international time scale, quantum sensors.

name: Two-Dimensional Photonics Fabrication Facility
description: HWU’s refurbished EPS Cleanroom has recently been enhanced with the integration of the new EPSRC Two-Dimensional Photonics Fabrication Facility, a unique world-leading facility capable of characterising two-dimensional materials and rapid fabrication of bespoke photonic devices. The 2D Photonics Fab facility is comprised of four main instruments to complement the array of existing cleanroom equipment. The "maskless" direct write laser is a lithographic tool to rapidly fabricate large scale bespoke 2D photonic devices with novel functionalities. It bridges the gap in lateral resolution and write speed between electron beam lithography and conventional photolithography (both also available) to enable rapid fabrication of nanoscale photonic devices over large areas. The lithography system is augmented by an electron beam evaporator enabling deposition of high-quality metals and dielectrics with nanometre precision onto devices lithographically defined by the direct write laser and a RIE/ICP plasma etcher. Finally, a spectroscopic imaging ellipsometer (EP4 from Accurion) is used. This unique instrument is crucial for characterization, either pre- or post-fabrication by the lithographic tool, of emerging two-dimensional semiconductor devices. The Facility is completed by a custom-designed glovebox with integrated optical characterization tools for the bespoke fabrication and initial characterization of robust and pristine 2D semiconductor heterostructures. With this setup, one can create new quantum devices with all degrees of freedom.

name: ATI Nano-Manufacture and Processing
description: The ATI Nano- Manufacture and Processing RII consolidates activities that design, process, fabricate, characterise and test novel materials and devices that rely on the nano-/micro-scale. This leads naturally to themes in quantum devices, energy harvesting and storage, communications (wireless & optical), and sensors. The ATI was originally formed as a joint venture between the EEE and Physics departments but welcomes users from all areas where the ATI’s facilities enhance their research. For example, some researchers from the Quantum Biology DTC are based in the ATI. The facilities are closely linked with the UK National Ion Beam Centre, for which Surrey is the lead partner.Formulationsallows limited synthesis of material, mainly perovskites and sol–gel precursors, concentrating on process for preparing materials as the solutions or dispersions needed for subsequent device fabrication.Fabricationat the sub-micron scaleuses both Gallium and plasma FIB, with a specialist ability to sculpt 3D patterns. This allows, for example, moulds for Fresnel micro-lens arrays. The ATI has produced SQUID devices for 20 years using this technology. It has extended to rapid prototyping of single electron transistor structures and is building on the single-ion implant capability for quantum devices. Most recently, an ion-beam technique that puts silicon, or germanium, under very high strain has been patented. This has the potential to create a direct band gap.At the micron scale the ATIclean room provides wet benches, plasma etching (RIE), physical vapour deposition (sputter by RF and DC, evaporation from thermal and ebeam sources.) A Heidelberg DWL66+ laser writer allows rapid prototyping, with Mode IV (2μm min feature) and Hi-res (300nm min feature) available. This tool will be used also to create templates to for use in the Nanoops FLEX RD printer, which is currently in final development and trials in the USA. Deposition of high-quality silicon oxide dielectric has been enhanced by the recent installation of atomic layer deposition.Nano-carbon materials(DLC, CNT and graphene) are supported by the plasma laboratory that has three planar CVD reactors, two of which embody the ATI’s photo-thermal CVD process, and a conventional LPCVD furnace. The ATI is currently working with an industrial client to scale-up a moisture-barrier for aerospace applications that relies on a combination of plasma CVD and a conventional vacuum-deposited material.Materials characterisationfacilities supporting these areas include: spectroscopic techniques: transmission and reflection in the near UV-vis-NIR range, FTIR, photoluminescence (PL) down to 10ps currently, with fs under development, and micro Raman; AFM; SNOM; Optical microscopy with 3D image stitching capability; electron microscopy (SEM) with EDX; xenon plasma FIB and gallium FIB; Talos F200i TEM; and 3D Dektak profilometry.Device characterisationfacilities allow electronic measurement in the range from dc to 67GHz. Facilities include: wafer probers, including a semi-automatic prober with multiple source—measure analysers; electrochemical interfaces/ potentiostats; battery testing; solar-simulators; locally-developed rigs for example: Triboelectric device testing and field emission characterisation. At high frequency we have RF on Wafer probing to 67GHz as part of a unique multi-physics microwave lab that includes high-speed thermal imaging and an electro–optic field probe.

name: Centre for Designer Quantum Materials
address: North Haugh, Scotland, KY16 9SS, United Kingdom
description: The Centre for Designer Quantum Materials at the University of St Andrews is a unique facility in the UK for the creation and study of new quantum materials. It allows the fabrication of new compounds with atomic-scale precision, where the composition of a material can be changed between each layer, effectively making entirely new “supermaterials” - combinations and structures which would be impossible to create by any other means. This is combined with unique research infrastructure for the characterization of the exotic quantum states that such materials can be designed to host. Together, this forms a powerful platform for the targeted design of functional materials, which can be expected in the long term to underpin a host of applications, from super-efficient energy distribution to high performance computing, sensing, and solid-state quantum technologies.The RII contains the following core components:A dual-chamber molecular-beam epitaxy (MBE) system optimised for the growth of complex quantum materials, in particular transition-metal oxides and chalcogenides. This capability allows the custom-engineering of materials for a host of applications as well as fundamental science studies, in particular via a direct connection to the spectroscopic tools of the CDQM;A suite of four low temperature scanning tunnelling microscopes enables characterization of quantum materials at the atomic scale, including imaging of their electronic states in real space and atomic-scale characterization of magnetic order. The microscopes are housed in the St Andrews ultra-low vibration labs, one of only a handful of such facilities worldwide, and operate at extreme conditions, including temperatures down to 7mK and magnetic fields up to 14T;A state-of-the-art capability for imaging the electronic structures of solids via laser-based angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy. This allows understanding how electrons move and interact in solids, key to their potential for use in devices and to unlocking the origins of the collective states of quantum materials. A new system is currently being developed within the CDQM to also enable spin-resolved measurements and microscopic focus, to yield new insights on designer magnets and spintronic compounds.A suite of magnetic and physical characterisation tools (MPMS/PPMS) including a bespoke nanoprobe instrument enabling local physical property measurements of thin film samples.Thin film XRD for rapid structural feedback on the growth.By working with other researchers and UK-based industry leaders in this area the CDQM is becoming established as a hub of Designer Quantum Materials in the UK and worldwide.

name: Centre for Sustainable Technologies
description: Ulster’s energy research Large Scale Energy System Analysis Eclipse developed by the University of Ulster is a techno-economic software tool utilising a mass-energy balance to determine capital costs and operating costs based on energy outputs, fuel costs and component costs. Electricity market modelling and simulation software that use for example stochastic optimisation techniques to integrate generation dispatch, unit commitment, optimal power flow and pricing simulations is utilised. Other models are used to determine the economic analysis of different energy related systems at a country scale over a period of usually of 40 to 50 years and LCA techniques are used for a range of future decarbonisation of electric power and industrial processes. Renewable Energy Bioenergy: Downdraft gasification, small scale anaerobic digestion and bio-oils are supported by a range of analysis tools to ascertain fuel quality, ash components, gases and energy. Solar Energy: A state-of-the-art simulator supports developments in PV concentrators and novel solar thermal energy storage. Optical characterisation delivers performance improvements. Energy Efficiency in Buildings and Industry and Advanced Vacuum Glazing: A low U-value vacuum gazed product with a metal edge seal is utilised both directly and as part of a standard double glazed unit to improve the thermal performance of buildings. Specially developed manufacturing and testing processes are also being used to advance new vacuum glazing based solar energy devices. High Temperature Heat Pumps: Air source heat pumps with novel compressors are developed that displace conventional boiler-driven hot water space heating systems thus reducing running costs and CO2 emissions. New working fluids and systems are also developed for industrial waste heat upgrading. Low Carbon Retrofit Test Houses: Local families live in heavily instrumented test houses that assess techno-socio-economic impacts of new technologies to reduce domestic energy costs. Energy Storage and Demand Side Management Thermal Storage Materials: Thermal storage for PV thermal management, solar thermal energy and heat pumps is developed using a range of heat exchange concepts that complement demand side management principles supporting larger scale renewable energy integration. Batteries for Distributed Energy Resources are under testing

name: Dimensional Metrology -  National Physical Laboratory
description: Dimensional metrology - providing expertise and capability in length and angle measurementMeasurement of 'length' includes distance, displacement, position, dimensions, size, area, volume and surface texture. It is a fundamental requirement in most engineering and manufacturing industries and a critical part in many science experiments and facilities. Together with measurement of angles, NPL's expertise in dimensional measurement covers over 12 orders of magnitude. From our highest resolution measuring system based on X-ray interferometry (20 picometre accuracy) to our large volume long range capability (10’s to 100’s of metres), we provide the fundamental length measurement infrastructure for the UK.We help organisations conform to precise dimensional requirements and help manufacturers develop, optimise and quality control their products. We have a range of bespoke facilities which means we are able to work with large objects that have to be measured in situ, whilst also having the ability to detect defects on the surface of extremely small parts. We have assembled and developed a range of contact and non-contact measuring systems. These range from X-ray tomography systems that allow examination of non-visible internal features, through laser interferometers for precise artefact calibration, to novel systems to provide factory floor coordinate measurement networks.At the pinnacle of length measurement, we realise the metre. We provide calibration services to disseminate the length scale to users in the UK. We develop standards, new methods and instruments, we support companies through bespoke developments, consultancy, training, and represent the UK on international measurement committees.NPL, as the UK's National Measurement Institute, is the prime deliverer of the UK Measurement Strategy. NPL is a world-leading centre of excellence in developing and applying the most accurate measurement standards, science and technology available. For more than a century, NPL has developed and maintained the nation's primary measurement standards. name: EPSRC Future Compound Semiconductor Manufacturing Hub
description: The CS Hub, based at Cardiff University, is one of 8 UK-wide EPSRC funded future manufacturing hubs. The CS Hub will act as the focal point for the creation of Europe’s 5th semiconductor cluster and the first dedicated to compound semiconductors. Our vision is to establish the UK as the primary global research and manufacturing hub for Compound Semiconductor (CS) Technologies. We are combining and connecting UK CS research excellence with the very best translational facilities to support the UK CS industry and UK industry users of CS. The hub and spoke universities will be linked to other centres of excellence throughout the UK. The combined activity will provide a path from enabling fundamental research through wafer, device and integrated chip manufacturing research into product prototyping, reliability testing and system qualification at the Compound Semiconductor Catapult. The EPSRC Manufacturing Hub in Compound Semiconductors will address the need to integrate CS and Silicon (Si) manufacturing, applying the manufacturing advances made in one type of CS across the different families of CSs and combine these different CSs for optimum functionality. Key goals 1) To radically boost the uptake and application of CS technology by applying the manufacturing approaches of Silicon to CS, 2) To exploit the highly advantageous electronic, magnetic, optical and power handling properties of CS while utilising the cost and scaling advantage of Silicon technology where best suited, and 3) To generate novel integrated functionality such as sensing, data processing and communication. Compound Semiconductor materials are a Key Enabling Technology underpinning the operation of the internet and enabling emerging megatrends such as Smart Phones, Internet of Things (IoT), satellite communications/GPS, energy efficient lighting, efficient solar power generation, advanced healthcare and ground-breaking biotechnology. The demands of next generation technologies are driving the integration / replacement of Silicon with advanced CS materials, creating a truly massive market opportunity for CS materials and devices.

name: IsoLab
address: Department of Physics, Lancaster University, North West, LA1 4YB, United Kingdom
description: IsoLab is a beyond state-of-the-art ultralow noise laboratory built as an annex to the Physics Department at Lancaster University. The build has been funded by the University and by substantial contributions from the Wolfson Foundation, the J.P. Moulton Charitable Foundation and the Garfield Weston Foundation. The EPSRC made an award for the first tranche of equipment and instrumentation. Within the facility sit three isolated laboratory spaces where vibration, noise and electromagnetic disturbance have been drastically reduced to give "ultra-clean" experimental environments for studying systems in controlled conditions. The capabilities are currently being exploited by researchers in quantum optics, extreme microscopy and low temperature solid state quantum technologies. IsoLab is used for the study of new phenomena, new material and devices, and drives innovation. It was designed to be used by Lancaster scientists along with external academic and industrial research partners. It provides access to advanced instrumentation and measurement capability, and training for current and future researchers.

name: Janet: the UK's Research & Education Network
description: Janet is the UK’s National Research and Education Network (NREN).  It connects all publicly-funded research and innovation (R&I) and is itself primarily publicly funded.  It also provides connectivity to privately-funded R&I as needed.    Janet is an essential underpinning of all R&I in the UK. Without it, no modern RII or collaboration could function. Jisc’s overall objective in providing Janet is to support the whole of the UK’s research and innovation sector by ensuring that Janet continues to meet the sector’s demands, particularly in the conduct of data-led R&I and collaborations.    The overall design aims of Janet with respect to R&I are threefold: to provide very high bandwidth and low-latency; to ensure high availability for mission-critical activities; and to provide leading-edge cybersecurity and information assurance. It achieves these aims via a purpose-built and operated optical platform which also provides a high degree of future-proofing against future demands. Janet operates across all countries of the UK.    Janet provides a range of discrete connectivity services. These include a high-performance and ubiquitous IP service; dedicated bandwidth and virtual private networks to support individual RIIs and projects; test-bed services including the EPSRC NDFIS and the NPIF-funded 5G test-bed; roaming services (eduroam); and higher information assurance services to facilitate the safe movement of sensitive data (Safe Share), particularly in medical and health research. Janet also provides high-bandwidth connectivity to the major cloud providers, an increasingly important source of compute and data storage services for R&I.    Janet is designed to provide global reach for UK R&I. It does so primarily through partnership with other NRENs and in particular through the EU GÉANT collaboration. This provides the means to project the services above on a European and worldwide scale.    Providing security requires a range of state-of-the-art technological measures and the expertise to deploy them to defend against the increasing cybersecurity threat landscape. As well as protecting the Janet infrastructure itself, the Janet Security Operations Centre (SOC) provides protection, threat analysis, advisory and other services to universities and others so that they may protect the integrity of the RIIs for which they are responsible. The SOC also works closely with UK law enforcement and national security agencies, and with international peers.

name: Long Beamline X-ray Test Facility
description: 27m beamline with a soft X-ray source and high energy X-ray tubes available providing an illuminated aperture of ~200mm diameter. The experiment chamber opens into an ISO 6 clean room and has both an imaging MCP detector (90mm x 90mm) and a CCD (27mm x 27mm). The detectors are mounted on a carriage with ~1m movement along the beamline and 165mm horizontal and 125mm vertical. A goniometer is available inside the chamber with ~arc second stability. Development, test and calibration of X-ray optics nominally <5keV. The facility has been used at higher energies in the past and operation at these energies is supported by 100kV tubes and appropriate shielding. There are no other such facilities in the UK and only a small number of similar facilities in Europe (Germany, Italy and Denmark). The UK has significant expertise in soft X-ray optics and access to such a facility is essential to allow groups to participate in international collaborations. Current facility is being/has been used to develop and calibrate x-ray optics for a number of space science missions the UK is involved in. Combined with instrument development for these missions this enables a science return to the UK academic community. With other wavelength sources facility can be used at other wavelengths. Sources for other wavelengths require refurbishment and testing or build and/or supply.

name: Quantum Computing and Simulation Hub
address: Clarendon Laboratory, University of Oxford, Parks Road, Oxford, South East, OX1 3PU, United Kingdom
description: The Quantum Computing and Simulation Hub(QCS Hub) is a collaboration between 17 UK universities and is one of four hubs within the UK National Quantum Technologies Programme.Working with an extensive network of academic, industrial and governmental partners, we are focused on the critical research challenges for quantum computing, across a broad range of hardware and software disciplines.As well as addressing the technical challenges in providing quantum computing and simulation at scale, the Hub engages with industries, end users, government and citizens to ensure the UK is 'quantum ready'. Our aim is to accelerate progress within quantum computing and ensure the UK becomes, and remains, a leader in the emerging global quantum information economy.PartnersThe partner institutions within the Hub are:University of BathUniversity of BristolUniversity of CambridgeCardiff UniversityDurham UniversityUniversity of EdinburghUniversity of GlasgowImperial College LondonUniversity of OxfordRoyal Holloway, University of LondonUniversity of SheffieldUniversity of SouthamptonUniversity of StrathclydeUniversity of SurreyUniversity of SussexUCLUniversity of WarwickResearchOur scientific research spans the full stack of hardware and software, made possible by the broad expertise of both our researchers and partners.  Our work on hardware involves developing scalable networks for both near-term and long-term quantum computers and simulators; our work on software and applications involves developing algorithms and protocols for how such machines could be used, as well as techniques to verify their operation.  We also study the architecture of quantum computers, and develop emulation techniques to allow future applications to be tested.Read more about our researchhere.Skills & trainingTogether with our partners, we are working to build the bridge between research and industry: we train researchers and PhD students in entrepreneurship, intellectual property and commercialisation - and also deliver training to technical, commercial and executive audiences to make sure the scientific potential of our work is understood and create opportunities for real-world, early adoption.EngagementClose engagement with a wide range of commercial and government organisations is crucial to both the success of the Hub and the future of quantum computing.  Our initial partners span across finance, suppliers, integrators and developers, users from industry, and government and other research institutions.We are working to nurture a quantum computing community in the UK and beyond by organising an engaging programme of events, including industry days, hackathons, and targeted workshops; we also deliver demonstrations, new algorithms, techniques, spinout technologies, and workforce skills training.We work closely with the National Centre for Quantum Computing, and continue to work with the global quantum computing community, policy-makers, and the wider public, to keep the UK at the forefront of the global quantum conversation.HeritageThe QCS Hub was preceded by the Networked Quantum Information Technologies Hub (NQIT) which ran from 2014-19.  Among its many achievements, NQIT developed a photonically-networked ion trap architecture, demonstrating node-node connectivity with a world-leading combination of rate and fidelity; it also set new benchmarks for the speed and precision of quantum logic operations.  Engaging with over 100 companies, NQIT encouraged and supported seven technology spinouts.  Building on the expertise and achievements of the previous hub, the QCS Hub began in late 2019 with a renewed ambition to create a quantum information economy in the UK.

name: Quantum Technology Innovation Centre
description: It is widely anticipated that quantum technology will affect everything from medical imaging to data security, from environmental sensing to how people engage with and use technology. The Quantum Technology Innovation Centre (QTIC) is the world's first dedicated quantum technology open access innovation facility for accelerating the development and adoption of quantum technologies. QTIC will facilitate engagement between scientists, industrial engineers and entrepreneurs; accelerate collaborative development and rapid prototyping of quantum devices, as well as other forms of IP; and, act as an early incubator for emerging quantum businesses. QTIC will ultimately be located within the University of Bristol’s new Temple Quarter campus, scheduled to open in September 2022, as part of its Digital Innovation Hub (DIH) facility. However, we have a pilot facility at One Cathedral Square, Trinity Street, Bristol, BS1 5TE. This facility, in addition to providing office space, meeting rooms and shared social areas to facilitate collaboration at 1 Cathedral Square, QTIC also provides two laboratories and a single ‘maker space’ to support the design, development and prototyping of quantum technologies. The electronics laboratories will enable members to develop and test sensing, imaging and communication technology prototypes, using a wide range of standard electronic equipment and software tools, including high magnification vision systems, infra-red viewing cameras, environmental chambers, and general electronics laboratory equipment. The maker space is a workshop where prototypes can be built with using traditional and advanced workshop tools including 3D printers, mini-mills, optical magnifiers, and hand tools. QTIC draws on established relationships with incubators across Bristol, such as the Engine Shed, SETSquared, Unit DX to provide support services, including coaching, mentoring, advice clinics, workshops, events, and investment readiness training, for developing and established businesses alike.

name: Small Research Facility for Sprays
description: The new Edinburgh Spray SRF includes several facilities for studying multi-phase fluid mechanics, even at high pressure (105 bar) and temperature (1000 K). The flows that are studied are mostly reactive sprays (e.g. for combustion or materials processing), but supercritical transitions and processing are also under study. What sets this SRF apart is the wide variety of measurement equipment available (in addition to the flow systems): rate of injection meters; phase Doppler interferometry; 2 high speed cameras (10,000 fps); 2 high speed pulsed Nd:YAG lasers (one is 2-pulse and one with all harmonics); a 2-pulse 10Hz Nd:YAG laser with dye laser and harmonics; a femtosecond, 1 kHz Ti:sapphire laser (using regenerative amplification); a picosecond 50 Hz Nd:YAG laser (using regenerative amplification); and ancillary equipment like an imaging spectrometer, a 6 W cw Nd:YAG at 532 nm, low light level scientific cameras, PIV (2-D and tomographic) and SLIPI optics and software, detectors, electronics and data sharing. This equipment enables a wide variety of measurement techniques including: nozzle interior flows (including cavitation); high-speed passive imaging (e.g. chemiluminescence or particulate luminescence); drop/particle size distributions and velocities in 2-D; stereoscopic and tomographic particle image velocimetry (PIV); planar laser induced fluorescence (PLIF) imaging of species concentrations or temperature; combined Mie scattering and PLIF for drops/vapour (or exciplex imaging); Raman scattering for major species; hybrid femtosecond/picosecond CARS for multi-dimensional temperature; interface and/or drop holography; structured laser illumination planar imaging (SLIPI); and ballistic imaging. The SRF is new and currently has no web page. This facility allows advanced experimentation on reactive mixtures, for combustion, materials processing, pharmaceutical production and so forth. No other UK facility offers the detailed measurements under so many different thermodynamic conditions that are available in this SRF.

name: Warwick Centre for Ultrafast Spectroscopy
description: The Warwick Centre for Ultrafast Spectroscopy (WCUS) is a state-of-the-art femtosecond laser facility comprising beamlines with experiments ranging from the ultraviolet range, through the visible and infrared, to the terahertz regime. This facility is run as a Research Technology Platform at the University of Warwick. The femtosecond, picosecond and nanosecond experiments possible are important to understand the dynamical motion of charges and vibrations in photophysical and photochemical systems. A suite of complementary optical spectroscopy equipment allow the equilibrium optical properties of materials to be determined.Research goals:Broadly, our research goals span four distinct areas: the use of lasers in medicine; the photostability of molecules; semiconductors and their optoelectronic properties; and quantum materials. In all cases, the use of femtosecond light pulses allows pump-probe measurements that track how a material behaves immediately after light is absorbed. name: Aero-Physics Laboratory and Facilities
description: The Aero-Physics Laboratory (APL) is a world-leading laboratory in aerodynamics, shock physics, wind tunnel testing, flow control and diagnostics. The laboratory embraces both scientific fundamental research and practical engineering applications. It has established major initiatives to deliver capability and technologies that contribute to the global aims of greener, faster and safer travel. The research covers the following four broad themes:Integrated wing technologiesthat involve: high Aspect Ratio wings; joined or non-planar wing concepts; load control via e.g. spanwise flow control adjustments; flow and flight control using active multi-functional materials; integrated system sensors and actuators with distributed control and health monitoring.Air-transport systems and space access: transition and SBLI; spacecraft plume characterization, contamination and fragmentation studies; plasma-based flow and flight control systems; integrated propulsion systems including intakes and ducts; advanced launchers.Aerospace vehicle design: design optimisation, uncertainty quantification, engineering analysis.Multi-disciplinary applications: e.g. combined PIV and PTSP imaging; nano-photonic sensors using plasmonic meta-materials; wind turbine and rotating wing technologies; inter-disciplinary shock wave research; discharges (dielectric, electrode, laser, microwave) and their interactions with surfaces and materials; plasma systems for science and engineering applications.APL is supported by an infrastructure which includes wind tunnels and bespoke testing facilities covering subsonic, transonic, supersonic, and hypersonic flow regimes:i)De Havilland Tunnel (National Wind Tunnel Facility): The De Havilland wind tunnel is one of nine UK strategic facilities, selected due to its significance and capability in performing a range of industry and academic relevant research projects.ii)ESA Plume-Regolith Testing Facility: This is one of the seven official ESA designated strategic facilities (Aero-Thermodynamics Directorate) distributed across Europe and is the only one that enables the realistic simulation of planetary conditions and testing of plume-regolith problems and interactions. The facility is aligned with ESA’s robotic Exploration Programmes requirements for missions including Mars Sample Return.iii)AWE Multi-Phase Turbulence Shock Tube: It is used to drive innovation in advanced propulsion systems and high-speed engine research with applications in the aerospace, nuclear, energy, safety, and security sectors.iv)Handley Page Wind Tunnel- test section: 2.1m x 1.5m; maximum speed 55m/s: It is equipped with a hydraulics system for unsteady aerodynamics research work and a force balance.v)High Speed Intake Facility- test section: 10 x 5 x 60cm (width x height x length); Mach number range M = 0.8 to 2.5: It is used to drive innovation in the design of high-speed intakes, scramjet engines and secondary flows injection systems.vi)Blowdown HSS Tunnel- Mach number range M = 0.8 to 3.0: It is one of the largest and most advanced university based high-speed wind tunnels.vii)Shock Tunnel- Mach number range M = 5 to 7: It can use Air, He or CO2 as the operating gas enabling the simulation of different planetary conditions.viii)S-Duct Testing Rig: This is a modular facility and it is used to drive innovations in propulsion systems and engine intake research.APL enjoys full access to a comprehensive set of instrumentation and flow diagnostic capability. APL is equipped with high speed schlieren and shadowgraph systems, double exposure holographic interferometry, hot-wire-anemometry, force balances, LDA, PIV systems, liquid crystals, PSP/TSP, IR thermography, LIF, spectrometers, variety of light sources, SEM/TEM characterisation capability and other standard instrumentation including high speed data logging and image acquisition systems. APL is also employing OpenFOAM for the numerical simulation of incompressible and compressible flows and in synergy with experiments. The partnership with HPC ARCHIE-WeSt (www.archie-west.ac.uk) allows for large scale computations to be performed.APL has generated new opportunities, and a step-change in our capability enabling new problems to be tackled (e.g. innovative design concepts, integration of modelling and flight simulation with wind-tunnel testing, novel flow and load control solutions for vehicle design and offshore applications). It drives innovation across a broad range of sectors including aerospace, process industries and wind energy. It is engaging with numerous organizations from the UK and abroad resulting in the generation of grants, and providing benefit from ready-access to scientific advances emanating. Affiliation with industry has enhanced pull-through to higher TRL. APL has already built an intellectual capability by the fostering of young talented researchers, and by attracting established academic groups from other universities. It is dedicated to supporting industry in developing their businesses, and generating high-value jobs, income and impact for the local and national economy (e.g. employment opportunities, development and training of the workforce, interaction with SMEs, and support for the supply chain).

name: BT Tower: London Urban Atmospheric Pollution Observatory
address: BT Tower, 60 Cleveland St, Greater London, W1T 4JZ, United Kingdom
description: PurposeThe purpose of the BT Tower London Observatory is to measure greenhouse gases and atmospheric pollutants and their fluxes (how they change over time) in an urban environment.  This long-term, high-precision monitoring provides valuable atmospheric chemistry data: to increase scientific understanding of atmospheric chemistry; to improve predictive models for air quality and greenhouse gas reduction; and to evaluate the effectiveness of policies to improve air quality and reduce greenhouse gases.CapabilitiesThe BT Tower Observatory provides long-term, high-precision, continuous measurement of airborne GHGs and pollutants.  By 2022  the measurement suite will comprise: carbon dioxide (CO2), methane (CH4), nitrous oxide (N2O), ethane (C2H6), carbon monoxide (CO), nitrogen dioxide (NO2) (all UKCEH) as well as nitric oxide (NO) and nitrogen oxides (NOx) (Univ. York / NCAS).   The eddy-covariance measurement system used at the BT tower consists of: a 3-D ultrasonic anemometer (R3-50, Gill Instruments; Reading Univ.); a dual quantum cascade laser absorption spectrometer (Aerodyne; UKCEH); an infrared gas analyser (LiCOR-7000, UKCEH); and a fast NOx analyser (Univ. York).  The anemometer is mounted on top of a lattice tower located on the roof of the BT tower giving an effective measurement height of 190m above street level.  The gas analysers are located a few floors below the roof, in an air conditioned room.  Air is sampled from ca. 0.3 m below the anemometer head at 20–25 L min-1 using a 45m long Teflon tube of OD 9.53mm (3/8'').  The observatory is also used for intensive measurement campaigns (for example in 2006, 2007, 2012 and 2021).LifecycleThe BT Tower Observatory was first established in 2006.  Its capabilities have developed and expanded since then, for example eddy-covariance measurements became routinely available from 2017.  UKCEH and BT have agreed a 10-year licence-to-occupy the Tower, to be reviewed in 2032.  The Observatory's core instrumentation was upgraded in 2021, providing a 10-year lifespan, also to be reviewed in 2032.  Other instrumentation is on a flexible replacement rota.UniquenessThe BT Tower Observatory is the only urban flux measurement site in the UK where urban emissions are being tracked long-term through direct measurement.PartnersUKCEH partners for the BT Tower observatory are: National Centre for Atmospheric Science (Atmospheric Measurement and Observation Facility); University of York; University of Reading.Location(s)BT Tower, Fitzrovia: 190 m above Central London.User(s)Data from the BT Tower Observatory is used by:  (1) Researchers interested in urban air quality fluxes;  (2) European Fluxes Database;  (3) Government as independent assessment of the effectiveness of emission reduction measures (e.g. ULEZ; NetZero policies).AccessResearch and observation outputs from the BT Tower Observatory are freely available via the Centre for Environmental Data Analysis: CEDA.  Physical access to the BT Tower Observatory, including capacity to add further instruments, is limited by space constraints: contact Eiko Nemitz, UKCEH Edinburgh.Funding SourcesThe BT Tower Observatory is currently funded by: the UKRI Strategic Priorities Fund (Clean Air programme and OSCA); NERC National Capability LTSS (UK-SCaPE programme); plus research project grants.

name: Chilbolton Observatory Space Monitoring Facility
address: Drove Road, Chilbolton, South East, SO20 6BJ, United Kingdom
description: The Chilbolton Observatory Space Monitoring Facility (COSMOF) provides a capability within the UK to monitor and characterise satellites and debris in orbit around the Earth. The facility is based at the UKRI-STFC Chilbolton Observatory because the principal sensors are located there. The 25m antenna at the Observatory supports a radar sensor called CASTR (Chilbolton Advanced Satellite Tracking Radar) that is used to track and range (measure the distance) satellites in LEO (Low Earth Orbit).  The recorded data can also be used to extract radar cross section information and inform about the situation of the target object (tumbling, polarisation signature).  The Observatory also operates an optical tracking system (COATS: Chilbolton Optical Advanced Tracking System) to track and observe objects in all orbital regimes around Earth (LEO, MEO, GEO, HEO).  COATS can accurately measure the angular position of the target object, and the recorded video data can also be used to analyse the situation of the target.  Accurate measurements of satellites and other objects in orbit around Earth enables their orbits to be determined with sufficient accuracy to propagate them into the future. This is critical to conducting accurate conjunction analysis (the process used to predict when two objects in orbit might collide with each other).  The sensors support UK and  international interests.The 25m antenna also supports a radio receiver that can be used for the In-Orbit-Testing (IOT) of satellites. This capability was initially developed to support the ESA testing campaign during the in orbit validation exercise of the European Galileo test bed satellites (GIOVE-A and GIOVE-B). This receiver system can be used to observe and characterise all GNSS satellites operating in the L-band. The sensors currently have an operational capability, but research is being conducted to improve the capability of the sensors. Additionally, the sensors will support research to improve the ability to further characterise the objects in space and their environment.The facility is important because it provides a unique capability in the UK to monitor and characterise satellites and debris in orbit around the Earth. The information gathered can inform analysts who assess the hazards due to object re-entry, object collisions, and object fragmentation. There are UK, European and international users of the data who currently benefit from the fact that the data is not given security classification.

Electron Paramagnetic Resonance (EPR) spectroscopy is possibly the most powerful technique for characterisation of paramagnetic materials, i.e. that contain unpaired electrons. Such materials are ubiquitous, from biological processes to magnetic materials; hence the technique is an essential tool in physics, chemistry, materials and biological sciences.    The National EPR Facility includes:  • multiple EPR spectrometers providing both continuous wave (c.w.) and pulsed EPR spectroscopy; frequencies ranging from 1 GHz to 34 GHz with arrangements in place for higher frequencies;  • facilities to measure samples directly in many different states (e.g. fluid solution, frozen solution, powder, single crystal, film, gas, melt, or even as a piece of biological membrane or within an electronic device) and at temperatures from ca. 2.5 to 400 K;  • skilled personnel to measure and to interpret spectra, and to design the best experiments for users;   • workshops to provide training from an introductory level suitable for new PhD students to advanced, to bring greater expertise to the wider user community;  • access to lasers, to allow characterisation of photo-excited states;  • complementary magnetic measurements.

name: European Magnetic Field Laboratory
description: Magnetic fields are powerful tools for studying the properties of matter and are essential for modern science. They were crucial for the ground-breaking research that led to 20 Nobel prizes in Physics, Chemistry and Medicine and will continue to underpin future scientific and technological developments by providing a powerful means of understanding and manipulating matter. In order to maintain their world-leading position in science, UK researchers require access to state-of-the-art magnetic field facilities and infrastructure. The cost of establishing and operating high magnetic field facilities is prohibitively expensive for individual Universities (> £50M). Moreover, the expertise required for building such facilities and for successfully running them is highly specialized. Thus in 2015, the major EU high magnetic field laboratories established a multi-site consortium, the EMFL. Within the EMFL, the HFML (operated by the Radboud University Nijmegen and the Netherlands Organisation for Scientific Research (NWO)) and the LNCMI-Grenoble (operated by the CNRS) are committed to generate the highest continuous magnetic fields, currently up to 37.5 T. The HLD-Dresden (operated by the Helmholtz-Zentrum Dresden-Rossendorf) and the LNCMI-Toulouse focus on non-destructive pulsed magnetic fields with strengths up to the feasibility limit of 100 T and semi-destructive fields up to 200 T. The facilities of the EMFL match those available in the USA, China and Japan, and offer opportunities for new experiments not yet available elsewhere. For example, magnetic fields can be used in conjunction with a free electron laser (FEL) at the HLD-Dresden (FEL-ELBE) and at the HFML-Nijmegen (FEL-FELIX Laboratory). The UK membership of the EMFL is funded by the EPSRC (2015-27). This has enabled the UK community to access the well-established facilities of the EMFL, to benefit from expert support from the local staff, and to establish joint activities. Funds from the EPSRC cover marginal costs for the UK access to the EMFL, including travel costs for the UK users. These are approximately half of the full running costs, i.e. they do not cover costs for the installations themselves or their depreciation. Since 2015 Prof. Amalia Patanè (University of Nottingham) has been a member of the EMFL Council to represent the UK community.

description: What it does:we are the national lead on aerial investigation and mapping and set the standards for the professional, academic and volunteer sectors. We undertake, manage and provide expert advice on programmes of aerial reconnaissance and air photo interpretation in order to discover, record, characterise and monitor archaeological sites, historic buildings, and landscapes. We use sources including historic and modern aerial photographs, satellite imagery and airborne laser scanning data (lidar) to produce archaeological maps linked to interpretations presented in national and local government geographical information systems (GIS) and textual databases. We use and promote multi-disciplinary survey including a variety of archaeological techniques (see other Historic England Infraportal entries). We also collaborate closely with our Architectural Investigation colleagues in urban and industrial survey and characterisation projects, notably in Heritage Action Zones.  We carry out research to create narratives exploring urban and rural change. We design and deliver specialist training including e-learning, classroom teaching and professional placements.Research/innovation goals and objectives:Developing efficient and effective tools for discovery of archaeological monuments including new in-air technology and innovative methods for predicting where sub-surface archaeology will show as cropmarks. Developing  best practice in the use of current and future sources including aerial photographs, visualisation of airborne laser scanning, sourcing and use of satellite imagery, multi-spectral imagery, and use of remote controlled-aircraft (drone) footage. Promoting and developing multi-disciplinary approaches to large area (100 square km plus) assessment to promote landscape based approaches for research and management.We contribute to guidance documents and to advice and training for the wider sector. We identify and promote work into major archaeological themes such as dating frameworks for site types or landscapes for unexcavated buried sites known from cropmarks. We develop innovative ways of sharing research results including developing Historic England’s digital offer by providing map-based (GIS) services to access digital aerial photographs, archaeological mapping and research reports.Why it is important:there is much that we do not know about England’s historic environment. name: ISIS Neutron and Muon Source
address: Rutherford Appleton Laboratory, Chilton, South East, OX11 0QX, United Kingdom
description: ISIS is the UK national facility for neutron scattering and muon spectroscopy, providing specialist research capabilities over a wide range of scientific disciplines, from life sciences to engineering. These techniques can only be provided by large central facilities; there are no laboratory-based equivalents. ISIS is an accelerator-based neutron/muon source; there are only 4 similar facilities operating in the world. Neutrons have a number of specific characteristics that makes them highly complementary to other experimental techniques such as X-rays/photons, NMR, microscopy etc. - a wide range of length and time scales, sensitivity to light atoms (e.g. H, Li, O), sensitivity to magnetism, high penetration allowing studies in complex environments, simple scattering process allowing direct connection to computer simulation/modelling, chemical selectivity through H/D contrast variation. As a national facility ISIS does not have its own research/innovation goals and objectives but responds as appropriate to the requirements of the scientific community and to national priorities. An increasing proportion of work includes an industry partner, usually in collaboration with universities. Applications include chemistry (e.g. catalysis), energy materials, soft matter e.g. polymers, materials engineering e.g. for aerospace, materials for healthcare, bioscience and heritage science.

name: Kelvin Hydrodynamics Laboratory
description: The Kelvin Lab houses a large hydrodynamics test tank (76m x 4.6m x 2.5m). equipped with state-of-art variable-water-depth wavemakers capable of generating long-crested waves of up to 0.7m height, an efficient wave-absorbing beach and a towing carriage capable of speeds of up to 4.5 m/s. The tank is well equipped with instrumentation including wave gauges, a wide range of force and pressure sensors, above-water and underwater optical motion capture systems and particle imaging velocimetry (PIV). The tank is used for research related to ships, offshore oil and gas platforms, and offshore renewable energy devices including fixed and floating wind turbines, tidal current devices and wave energy convertors. Fundamental hydrodynamic research focusses on highly non-linear/unsteady flows and fluid structure interaction phenomena such as prediction of safety and through-life integrity of offshore structures due to peak loads in exceptional storms (e.g. 1-in-100 year storms), accumulated fatigue damage due to storms which occur several times per year, behaviour of floating structures in extreme waves, aero-hydrodynamic response of floating wind turbines and flow around compliant structures including vortex-induced vibration. The facility also supports Ocean Engineering research topics including ship and offshore structure performance, as well as production of benchmark data for CFD / FSI code validation.The laboratory also houses a compact wave tank (9m x 3.2m x 1m) with optical access, capable of generating multi-directional waves. The tank was commissioned in 2020, and is suitable for fundamental research work and low TRL studies. A fully turbulent flow channel (FTFC) equipped with high-speed stereo PIV is also available at the Kelvin Lab; this facility is sutiable for investigation of near wall flows in fresh and salt water.

description: The Liverpool Telescope (LT) is a 2.0 metre fully robotic telescope owned and operated by Liverpool John Moores University.  The telescope is sited at the Observatorio del Roque de Los Muchachos on the Spanish Canary Island of Palma at an altitude of 2363 metres.  Operating costs are partially supported by STFC.  The telescope specialises in time domain astrophysics, with rapid reaction to targets of opportunity (e.g. Gamma Ray Bursts and Supernovae) and the capability of monitoring variability on timescales from seconds to decades.  The telescope is equipped with optical (visible) and near-infrared instrumentation, providing imaging, polarimetric and spectroscopic capabilities.  Time on the telescope for external users is available through the UK Panel for Allocation of Telescope Time.

name: Medium Energy Ion Scattering Facility
address: Queensgate, North West, HD1 3DH, United Kingdom
description: MEIS is a high-resolution depth profiling tool for the structural and compositional characterisation of nanolayers as well as for the determination of atom location and layer registry. In a MEIS experiment a collimated beam of mono-energetic 100 - 200 keV light ions (H+ or He+) impinges onto a target and projectiles undergo collisions with target atoms at any depth during penetration. The energy and angle of ions that are backscattered are measured simultaneously with a high-resolution, state-of-the-art electrostatic energy analyser equipped with a position sensitive detector. From the 2-D energy-angle spectra recorded, the elemental mass and the depth of the scattering event below the surface is determined with a depth resolution down to 1 nm. This ability makes it a highly valuable tool in the analysis of shallow layers, either ion implanted or grown nanolayers, by e.g. chemical vapour deposition (CVD) or atomic layer deposition (ALD). Moreover, for crystalline samples using ion beam channelling and blocking, MEIS can determine the surface atomic arrangement, reconstruction, as well as the registry of overlayers and the position of adatoms. The importance of the MEIS facility lies in its capability of providing this combined high depth resolution compositional and structural information not easily available from other analytical techniques. The combination of MEIS with a Surface Science station in one interlinked UHV system make the Huddersfield set up a powerful facility, unique in the UK or indeed in Europe where there are only two operational MEIS systems. In its previous incarnation in STFC Daresbury Laboratory, the MEIS facility vitally underpinned fundamental and applied research of some 30 UK and overseas research groups in areas such as surface interactions in catalysis, surface adsorption and reconstruction, metal-metal overlayer structures, high-K oxide dielectric film formation, oxidation, anodic film growth, corrosion protection and the characterisation of implants for shallow junctions, disorder formation and annealing in semiconductors. The re-establishment and expansion of this role has been the main research/innovation objective since its reopening at Huddersfield University in 2015. Current research is focussed on the characterisation of Plasma Doping processes, Transparent Conductive Oxides produced by novel laser ablation processes, electron emitting surfaces for photo cathodes, bimetallic catalytic surfaces and doped AlGaAs nanonolayers.

description: The n3m labs is the world's first multi-physics enabled laboratory dedicated to nonlinear microwave measurements & modelling. The facilities combine multi-physics (RF, low-frequency electrical, and thermal) modelling using commercial software tools on a high-performance computing cluster with measurement. The electrical measurement capabilities cover the range to 67 GHz at the University of Surrey (extending to 750 GHz at NPL). The n3m-labs combines the non-linear vector networks analysis with vector load-pull and with transient thermal measurement and electro to optic e-field probing so that there is a responsive multi-physics measurement capability to inform modelling. name: Perceptual Experience Laboratory
description: Since 2011 FovoLab has been developing new ways to depict the world on two-dimensional images to the audience of the future - The Perceptual Experience Laboratory (PEL) represents the cutting edge of that work. PEL is a unique, goggle-less mixed-reality user testing environment that can be configured to accurately represent virtually any space in terms of sight, sound, smell, temperature and air movement.    The facility can be configured in as little as half an hour.     PEL was developed by two research groups: FovoLab and User Centred Design Research (UCD-R). It is comprised of a 2.5 metre high, 5K resolution cylindrical rear projection screen wrapped 200° around the participant. Smell, environment temperature, three-dimensional sound, air movement, digital projection, physical objects and actors are used to emulate environments realistically at a multi-sensory level, while body movements, eye movements, heartbeat variability and galvanic skin responses can all be monitored via state of the art user observation systems including timecoded video and audio recording from multiple sources. PEL can simulate virtually any environment in laboratory conditions. Its uses include medical product testing, diagnostic testing of vision conditions, testing in dangerous environments, remote testing scenarios etc. PEL offers immersion levels literally impossible in any other facility. Its ultimate goal is the development of a new type of VR.    Accurately portraying the 3D world in 2D is one of humans’ oldest quests. FovoLab’s work in non-linear perspective imagery represents the new front line in this quest. Linear perspective - discovered in the early fifteenth century - remains the basis of most imaging technologies, including photography, cinematography, computer aided design, video gaming, x-rays, VR and AR. Elegant as it is, linear perspective models how light travels, not how humans experience and record visual experiences. Anyone who has ever tried to photograph the majesty of a beach or the grandeur of a cathedral will know that photographic depiction is no substitute for visual experience. This is because the multi-sensory human perceptual system captures and processes far more information than can be captured in an optically-derived photograph. E.g., the proportions and layout of our visual field change according to our chosen point of awareness, a reason why many artists create paintings with a single intended fixation point.

name: Rock Volume Characterisation Laboratory Cluster (British Geological Survey)
address: British Geological Survey, Keyworth, East Midlands, NG12 5GG, United Kingdom
description: The Rock Volume Characterisation Cluster studies the structural and compositional characterisation of rocks and their constituent parts, from micron to kilometre scale. The cluster comprises a set of leading analytical capabilities and expertise, outlined below, that focus on the characterisation of materials at a range of scales and target different attributes (mineralogy, geochemistry, structure etc). Current activities in the cluster are focused on the integration of the individual capabilities in order to offer a more complete, coherent characterisation for a range of geological applications.  The cluster is comprised of four facilities/areas of specialism:Core Scanning Facility (CSF) - The facility is centered on a suite of core scanners, including two multi-core XRF scanners, an X-Ray CT scanner and multi-sensor core logger. Combined, these analytical techniques are non-destructive and are used to characterise the chemical and physical properties of rock cores from around a hundred microns up to kilometre scale, providing data to underpin a breadth of science applications.  The CSF was established in 2018 as part of the BEIS-fundedUK Geoenergy Observatories project (UKGEOS)and characterisation of materials associated with the project is the initial focus on this new facility, providing information that underpins subsurface studies in regard to use of the subsurface with respect to energy, waste and resources.Geochronology and Tracers Facility (GTF) - GTF is part of the NERC-fundedNational Environmental Isotope Facility(NEIF), in addition to being a BGS facility. This group specialises in using a range of isotopic analyses of rocks and minerals for the dating of rocks and minerals and as ‘tracers’ for a breadth of geological and environmental processes, using a combination of approaches using both spatial resolution and high-precision techniques. The chronology capabilities are applied to a range of materials, from traditional uranium-bearing minerals to less commonly utilised phases such as carbonate, allowing for a wider range of applications. The same analytical capabilities are used for the application of tracer isotopes (e.g., Si, Sr, Nd, Pb) to a range of geological, environmental and archaeological science topics.Mineralogy and Petrology - Mineralogy and petrology is an established set of capabilities at BGS, from bespoke thin-section preparation though optical microscopy, scanning electron microscopy and X-ray diffraction to thermal analysis and allied techniques. These methods allow for the identification and quantification of the mineralogy and petrology of rocks, soils and particulates. This information is used to underpin a wide range of materials characterisation and research, including diagenesis, mineralisation and fluid/rock interaction.Palaeontology and Stratigraphic Volumes - The BGS Palaeontology Laboratories are part of a broader capability in palaeontology in BGS, which also includes extensive collections of macrofossils and microfossils. Our palaeontology laboratories constitute essential sample preparation facilities in palynology and micropalaeontology for a range of stratigraphic uses. The data is combined with other stratigraphic data types (e.g. chemistry and physical properties) to help characterise ‘stratigraphic volumes’ that are targets for use in understanding the properties of such volumes at the scales from microns to kilometres.

name: Roland von Glasow Air-Sea-Ice Chamber
address: University of East Anglia, School of Environmental Sciences, East of England, NR4 7TJ, United Kingdom
description: What is does: The Roland von Glasow Air-Sea-Ice Chamber (RvG-ASIC) facility at the University of East Anglia (UEA) comprises a coupled atmosphere–ocean–sea-ice simulation chamber designed to investigate the role of first-year sea-ice in tropospheric chemistry. The temperature of the chamber can be controlled within a range from –55 to +30 °C, with a stability of ±0.3 °C. The chamber can also be run in ocean-atmosphere mode (above –2 ºC), snow-atmosphere mode (no liquid water) or in dry mode for purely atmospheric investigations. It provides a platform for a diverse range of multi-disciplinary experiments to study physical, chemical and biological between interactions between atmosphere, ocean, ice and snow.Research/innovation goals and objectives: The RvG-ASIC facility is a purpose built facility designed to investigate research questions involving ocean-sea-ice-atmosphere interaction, e.g. sea-ice physics and optics, chemical uptake of pollutants into sea-ice, bacterial populations in sea-ice, release of trace gases from ice under UV irradiation. It is part of UEA's Centre for Ocean and Atmospheric Sciences (COAS).Why it is important: The Arctic Ocean is changing rapidly, with multi-year ice becoming far less important and fresh, seasonal sea-ice becoming the norm. Working on natural sea-ice is challenging, expensive and at times dangerous. It is a complex environment, with little control on experimental conditions and difficult access. The RvG-ASIC facility aims to remove those barriers and provide an environment suitable for replicating conditions seen in polar oceans. In its ability to combine water, ice and atmospheric phases, the chamber is unique in the world.

name: Rothamsted Field Scanalyzer
address: West Common, Harpenden, East of England, AL5 2JQ, United Kingdom
description: This is the world’s first field crop analytics facility supported by Rothamsted Research and BBSRC. The Field Scanalyzer comprises of a gantry that supports a motorised measuring platform with multiple sensors. Crops within a 15m x 120m area can be monitored throughout the season with a high degree of resolution and reproducibility. The facility is fully automated and can operate 24 hours per day throughout the year. On board illumination facilitates the data collection and sensors for simultaneous and non-destructive analyses of plant growth, morphology and physiology. Together, the data obtained from the panel of sensors will provide a complex picture of plant growth and vigour from single field experiments. The Field Scanalyzer is robust enough to cope with harsh environmental conditions with high sampling frequency and greenhouse accuracy, which provides information concerning the varietal responses to the extrinsic environment and their effect on yield. CAMERAS AND SENSORS Rothamsted’s Field Scanalyzer is equipped with the most complete high-resolution sensor array that may be accurately positioned in three dimensions and mounted on fixed rails. The platform facilitates the continual and high-throughput monitoring of performance and health of field grown crops. Employed sensors comprise of high-resolution visible, chlorophyll fluorescence and thermal infrared cameras, two hyperspectral imagers, and dual 3D laser scanners. The sensor array facilitates specific growth measurements and identification of key growth stages with dense temporal and spectral resolution. Together, this platform produces a detailed description of canopy development and health across the crops entire lifecycle, with a high-degree of accuracy and reproducibility.

description: Unique to the UK, the Royce Translational Centre (RTC) is a Centre for Smart Specialisations in Metal Alloy Powder-to-Part Manufacture and builds on existing University of Sheffield’s expertise in advanced powder processing and the Sheffield City Region’s (SCR) capacity in advanced manufacturing. Materials development for engineering applications has been identified as a key technological area in which the UK needs to invest in to maintain its position as a global player in advanced manufacturing and engineering. A burgeoning area of materials development is powder metallurgy, a global market that is expected to reach $8.7B by 2020, as it provides the material feedstock for many key industrial sectors (aerospace, automotive, energy, medical, high value manufacturing) and manufacturing processes (Metal Injection Moulding, Hot Iso-static Pressing & 3D printing). The University of Sheffield has an established heritage in both powder processing and industrial translational activities. These industrial translational activities have highlighted a supply chain gap which is emerging between the provision of metal powder and its subsequent implementation in manufacturing processes. Early adopters, primarily OEMs in the aerospace and Formula 1 sectors, are able to finance substantial research costs internally, and can therefore develop new alloy powders and novel part manufacture and post-processing techniques. Secondary adopters, particularly SMEs in the commercial automotive supply chain, medical, energy or construction sectors neither have the resources nor expertise to take advantage of these developments and as such may become uncompetitive in a global market. The RTC, located on the Advanced Manufacturing Campus, acts as an open access centre of excellence by incorporating both existing and newly purchased state of the art powder manufacture and processing facilities in a bespoke facility, raising the profile and consolidating regional metal powder expertise, that will attract SME’s and large enterprises, with the aim of stimulating innovation to drive supply and value chain development.

name: SPIN-Lab
description: SPIN-Lab is a state-of-the-art hub of magnetic characterisation at Imperial College London to address the needs of our growing user base across the London area, consolidate the community, generate new cross-discipline cutting-edge science on spin-related phenomena and enable more ambitious research programmes to be developed. The facility includes equipment for the study of strongly coupled and isolated spins through the investment in new, unique instruments and the upgrade of existing tools. Key new instruments include a SQUID/VSM-based magnetometer operating up to 7 Tesla, with high sensitivity (10^-7 emu), and low-temperature capabilities (1.8 K). It will have a range of options making it unique in the UK: (i) high-temperature capability (up to 1000 K), (ii) hydrostatic pressure cells (not possible with vibration-based magnetometers), (iii) ultra-low field conditions: ± 0.05 G with a 7 T magnet (the current instrument at UCL operates with ± 10 G), (iv) a fibre-optic sample holder, and (v) an AC susceptibility option. In the low coupling regime, the facility includes a Bruker X-Band CW-EPR Spectrometer System, the ELEXSYS E500-10/12 SYSTEM. The CW operation will enable high sensitivity down to 1.0 x 109 spins. The system will be fitted with a cryostat for operation down to 4K, and be integrated with a pulsed laser for the study of transient phenomena. The facility also includes add-ons to PI-led equipment, such as ferromagnetic resonance, solid-state NMR, external magnetic field vectors for atomic force microscopy, and variable temperature operation on a Hall probe system. The integrated approach is unique in the UK. SPIN-Lab aims to generate a step-change in research output by introducing/fully harnessing a new degree of freedom in the existing portfolio of world-class activities in materials fabrication and characterisation currently going on at Imperial and across the UK.

name: Sorby Environmental Fluid Dynamics Laboratory
description: The Sorby Environmental Fluid Dynamics Laboratory (SEFDL) is a NERC recognised facility hosted by the University of Leeds and provides a unique and world-leading array of facilities for the study of environmental fluid dynamic problems. Users of the facility have access to a wide range of different flume facilities and pipe loops, and a unique suite of measurement equipment. These include laser and phase Doppler anemometry, multiple particle image velocimetry (PIV) systems capable of running at up to 3000 Hz, and a number of ultrasonic Doppler velocimetry systems. SEFDL is in the process of adding volumetric PIV and 3D laser Doppler anemometry to its suite of instrumentation. Additionally, SEFDL possesses a broad range of instrumentation for measuring other variables such as rheological parameters, concentration, grain-size, salinity, temperature, flow structure and deposit morphology.We have a state-of-the-art suite of instrumentation for measuring grain-size from nanoscales through to millimetric, coupled to particle shape analysis for both wet and dry samples, using a range of in situ and ex situ techniques. This capability can be used to better quantify sediment character, including pollutants. Some of the measurement facilities are available as an equipment pool for loan to suitably equipped laboratories. Coupled to these facilities, the SEFDL provides access to considerable expertise in the design of experimental facilities, the application and optimisation of fluid measurement techniques, and the post-processing of data collected from such instruments. In particular, the SEFDL has pioneered the application of a number of these techniques to the field of environmental fluid dynamics, most notably for opaque, sediment-laden, and multiphase flows and consequently can provide extensive expertise in these areas.Increasingly, SEFDL provides validation data for sophisticated numerical models; techniques such as PIV are ideally suited to providing spatial and temporal datasets that can be compared to computational fluid dynamic models. SEFDL aims to support the numerical modelling community in the environmental sciences as extensive validation of models becomes routine. The SEFDL also has access to both excellent mechanical and electrical workshop facilities in order to construct specific user-defined apparatus.

description: The MIF in Central London provides convenient access to methods for monitoring bio molecular interactions in solution, namely analytical ultracentrifugation (three Beckman analytical ultracentrifuges, one being the new multiwavelength Optima, and two older XLI instruments, one with fluorescent optics), surface plasmon resonance (Biacore X100), and dual polarisation interferometry (local), and we access the X-ray and neutron scattering beam lines at ISIS, Diamond, ILL and ESRF.
