name: UK Electronprobe Microanalysis
description: EPMA is a non-destructive technique yielding high-precision elemental composition data of materials at micro- and nanometre scales. This union of analytical precision and spatial resolution makes EPMA unique among microanalytical techniques. Its data contributes to a variety of disciplines critical to the UK research and industrial infrastructure. These include fields as diverse as photonics, earth science resources, nuclear engineering, and high strength, high-temperature materials development, archaeology and biomaterials. Users of these facilities include academic researchers as well as industrial partners such as Rolls Royce, EDF and mineral resource companies. The technique is relatively mature, yet the last decade has seen significant innovation in technique simulation and instrumentation. These have led to increases in analytical resolution, and type of chemical data acquired. They offer significant benefits to end-users, particularly those involved in time-dependant processes, such as materials oxidation, and slow, diffusion controlled processes which demand the highest spatial resolution. The UK has some 20 installations shared between academia and industry working on a variety of materials. The RII connects a network of 14 installations in 6 universities (Oxford, Cambridge, Bristol, Leeds, Manchester, Edinburgh), 2 government bodies, (Natural History Museum, AWE), and Johnson-Matthey (NB. data for last two not included). Our goals are four-fold: To advance EPMA as a fundamental technique of advanced materials characterization; to continue to capture innovations that will deliver the world-class data demanded by our academic and industrial partners To underpin teaching of the next generation of materials scientists in the field of EPMA, in particular the correct interpretation of data To raise the awareness of funding bodies of who we are, what we do and, crucially, what we can offer the UK R&D community To highlight the centres of microanalytical excellence that exist in UK institutions to both UK Industry and other global bodies. The UK has made significant investments in the technique, but the community needs a forum to plan the roadmap for continued innovation, education and knowledge exchange. The RII was a new initiative formed in 2018.

name: University of Bristol Flow Cytometry Facility
description: The Flow Cytometry Facility, housed in the Faculty of Life Sciences in the Biomedical Sciences Building at the University of Bristol, includes instrumentation for analyses and sorting of cells and particles. The facility has extensive experience with sorting of a wide range of cell types, and with cloning and index sorting of rare cell populations.   The facility plays a critical role for research in areas including immunology, cancer biology, regenerative medicine, microbiology, cardiovascular medicine, chemistry and synthetic biology. Experimental design, data analysis, high dimensional cell phenotyping are some of the tasks undertaken by the facility.  The fluorescence activated cell sorting platform includes two Becton Dickinson high speed cell sorters:   An Influx high speed sorter equipped with 4 lasers and 16 optical detectors. The Influx sorter can perform up to 6 way sorting and also has the Small Particle Option for detection and sorting of sub-micron particles.  An Aria II SORP sorter equipped with 4 lasers and 18 optical detectors capable of 4 way sorting.    The cytometry analyser suite houses Becton Dickinson FACS Canto II (2 lasers, 6 colours), LSR II (3 lasers and 10 colours) and Fortessa X20 (4 lasers, 16 colours) instruments, and the 13 colour Acea Novocyte 3000. These cytometers are routinely used for multi-colour staining analyses and fluorescent protein reporter experiments.    A Luminex 200 system is used for multiplex analysis of cytokines, phosphoproteins and other analytes.    An Amnis Imagestream imaging cytometer will be installed in 2019

name: University of Edinburgh BioImaging small research facility
description: The aim of the BioImaging Facility is to investigate new imaging technologies and to provide access to state-of-the-art imaging instrumentation to users from biology and material sciences. The facility specialises in label free microscopy comprising of three core techniques Bio-Atomic force microscopy, Raman spectroscopy and multi-photon label free imaging (CARS/SRS/ SHG). Label free microscopy techniques are less invasive than fluorescence techniques, as the chemical composition and structure of the sample can be directly investigated without changing the sample by adding a stain or other invasive processing methods. Despite the advantages of these methods, they have so far been limited to use by specialised research labs, this facility provides access to these techniques to a wider user base across the UK.

name: University of Leeds AFM Facility
description: The Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) Facility in the School of Physics and Astronomy consists of 7 state-of-the-art instruments with a capital value of £1.9M. Founded in 2005, it is co-managed by Dr Simon Connell (SDC) and Dr Neil Thomson (NHT) who both have over 20 years experience in AFM, and it currently operates as a Small Research Facility (SRF) in the MAPS Faculty. SDC is mostly responsible for the operation of the lab. It has steadily grown in size and number of users, and houses a range of ambient AFMs, optimised for different imaging modes and force measurements. Research is highly interdisciplinary, supporting both discovery and challenge-led research in a wide range areas: Biophysics and soft matter, biomaterials and tissue engineering, magnetism and magnetic materials, spintronics, polymer materials, particle technology, surface science and synthetic biology. It’s main specialisms are soft matter structure, dynamics and mechanics, biological and aqueous imaging, general materials characterisation and magentic imaging. It is widely recognised as a leading AFM centre in the UK, resulting in UoL hosting two major UK AFM conferences this coming year (Bruker AFM User Meeting in October 2017 and UK SPM in June 2018) as well as 2013, 2006 and 2001.  The two newest Instruments include a Bruker FastScan (Wellcome Trust 101497/Z/13/Z: £343,717), and a correlative AFM-confocal microscope (BB/R000174/1: 2018, £600,000) unique to the UK. Additionally there have been recent major hardware upgrades to two existing Multimodes, the FastScan, a major upgrade to an MFP-3D AFM to the Bio specification by integration with an inverted optical microscope, two new environmental controllers and a variable magnetic field module. This total investment in the last five years is £1.2M. It supports research across UoL, in the Faculties of MAPS, Engineering, Medicine and Biological Sciences, and is heavily utilised by four current EPSRC CDTs (CP3, SOFI, MSE and TERM). It provides a consultancy service, and is involved with many industrial projects, via fully funded PDRA’s, or through funded PhD research studentships. These include Pfizer, PepsiCo, Johnson Matthey, grant funded research with Unilever, and smaller projects with Astra-Zeneca and Syngenta.It is a nationally important UK Facility, with potential to become internationally leading.

name: Versatile X-ray Spectroscopy Facility
description: X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is a fundamental tool for characterising the chemical composition of condensed matter, both in the bulk and at the surface of samples. Conventional XPS instrumentation was based on ultra-high vacuum characterisation, so volatile or aqueous samples were difficult to examine. Through funding from the Royce Institute, we have acquired a bespoke laboratory instrument for XPS for the characterization of materials under prcatically relevant conditions, from natural environmental conditions to industrial chemical process conditions. It enables measurement of samples and devices containing volatile components (e.g. solvents, adsorbed gases, biological and biomaterials, pastes and suspensions) without cryogenic cooling. Self-compensating charge neutralisation through the gas phase permits facile investigations of electrically insulating samples, studies of whole devices and dynamic processes, and the depth profiling of molecular materials, all under environmental conditions. Through the Diamond-Leeds Collaboration the facility can engage users with more advanced X-ray core level spectroscopy characterisation at the versatile soft X-ray spectroscopy (VERSOX) beamline at Diamond Light Source, the UK’s national synchrotron facility. The facility also variable photon energy X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (under vacuum conditions) with Cr K, Ag L, Zr L, Al K, Cr L and Zr M sources.

name: Western Channel Coastal Observatory
description: The Western Channel Observatory (WCO), established in 2005, essentially brought under one umbrella the time-series stations at L4 (50° 15?N, 4° 13.2?W; 50 m depth) which is situated 13 km off Plymouth, and E1 (50° 02?N, 4° 23?W; 75 m depth) situated 40 km offshore (see Fig. 1), together with modelling and remote sensing expertise (see http://www.westernchannelobservatory.org.uk). The inner site L4 is dynamic and tidally influenced, receiving periodic floodwater influence from the nearby rivers Tamar and Plym. However, like the E1 site it stratifies consistently in summer, with a pronounced thermocline and nutrient depletion (Smyth et al., 2010). This is important because many other high resolution, comprehensively sampled long time series sites in Europe are permanently mixed or close to shore. L4 forms part of the ICES network of time series sites across the North Atlantic seaboard (O’Brien et al., 2013) and is a “sentinel” monitoring site for the transitionally stratified regimes, under the European Marine Strategy Framework Directive. The marine environment off the coast of Plymouth (UK) has been monitored routinely since the latter decades of the nineteenth century. The wide range of observations, from physical parameters such as photons to biological ones such as fish, gives marine scientists the ideal opportunity to carry out contextualised process studies, develop and validate ecosystem models and remote sensing algorithms, as well as to quantify the extent and impact of climatic and environmental change. It also allows the relative impact of natural and anthropogenic drivers to be assessed, thus contributing to fundamental discussions at the national and international level on marine ecosystem change.
